ECHS

AP Calculus 5.10

Optimization • Modelling • Geometry • Economics • Applied Problems

AP CALCULUS 5.10

Optimization Problems

LESSON MAP
01

Compact Optimization Method

A short procedure for modelling, domain selection, differentiation, and conclusion.

3 concise slides
PROCEDURE

Optimization in Four Moves

1Define

Choose the variable and the quantity to maximize or minimize.

2Model

Use the constraint to write the objective in one variable.

3Restrict

State the physical domain before solving the derivative equation.

4Verify

Compare admissible critical points and endpoints; answer with units.

MODELLING

Objective, Constraint, and Domain

Objective

The area, volume, cost, distance, revenue, time, or angle being optimized.

Constraint

The equation connecting the variables.

One-variable form

Substitute the constraint into the objective.

Physical domain

Require every length, radius, quantity, and denominator to be valid.

CHECKLIST

Before You State the Answer

  • Is the objective written in one variable?
  • Is the physical interval correct?
  • Were critical points and relevant endpoints compared?
  • Did you return to the requested dimensions, price, time, or cost with units?
SECTION 01
03

Level 1 — Core Optimization Models

Introductory algebraic, geometric, box, distance, solid, and economic models.

47 questions
QUESTION 1

Minimum Tangent Slope

Question 1Level 1
Find the x-coordinate at which the tangent slope to y=13x3−3x2−40 is as small as possible.
Open interactive graph and model
Show complete solution

The slope function is m(x) = y′ (x) = x2 − 6x = (x − 3)2 − 9. This quadratic has its minimum at x = 3.

Model and calculus verification

Tangent slope

Objective and domainf(x)=x(x-6)F{\left(x \right)} = x \left(x - 6\right)
-2≤x≤8-2\le x\le 8
Differentiateⅆⅆxf(x)=2x-6\frac{d}{d x} F{\left(x \right)} = 2 x - 6
Compare admissible candidates
xF
-2-21616
33-9-9
881616
Verified min: x=3x = 3. The interactive diagram and graph use this same objective.
Open persistent student workspace
QUESTION 2

Least Acceleration

Question 2Level 1
A particle moves according to s(t)=t4−12t3+78t2−6t+30, where s is in metres and t in seconds. Find the least acceleration.
Open interactive graph and model
Show complete solution

Velocity and acceleration are v(t) = s′ (t) = 4t3 − 36t2 + 156t − 6, a(t) = v′ (t) = 12t2 − 72t + 156 = 12(t − 3)2 + 48. The square term is nonnegative, so the acceleration is minimized at t = 3.

Model and calculus verification

Acceleration

Objective and domainf(t)=12t2-72t+156F{\left(t \right)} = 12 t^{2} - 72 t + 156
0≤t≤80\le t\le 8
Differentiateⅆⅆtf(t)=24t-72\frac{d}{d t} F{\left(t \right)} = 24 t - 72
Compare admissible candidates
tF
00156156
334848
88348348
Verified min: t=3t = 3. The interactive diagram and graph use this same objective.
Open persistent student workspace
QUESTION 3

Rectangular Garden of Maximum Area

Question 3Level 1
You have 40 ft of fencing for a rectangle. Find the largest area and the dimensions.
Open interactive graph and model
Show complete solution

A ′ ( x ) = 20 − 2 x = 0 ⇒ x = 10 Compare A(0)=0, A(10)=100, A(20)=0. The maximum is 100 ft² for a 10 ft × 10 ft square.

Model and calculus verification

Area

Objective and domaina(x)=x(20-x)A{\left(x \right)} = x \left(20 - x\right)
0≤x≤200\le x\le 20
Differentiateⅆⅆxa(x)=20-2x\frac{d}{d x} A{\left(x \right)} = 20 - 2 x
Compare admissible candidates
xA
0000
1010100100
202000
Verified max: x=10x = 10. The interactive diagram and graph use this same objective.
Open persistent student workspace
QUESTION 4

Fixed Area Rectangle

Question 4Level 1
A rectangular plot has area 400 m². Find the smallest possible perimeter.
Open interactive graph and model
Show complete solution

Let the sides be x and y. Since xy = 400, we have y = 400/x.

Model and calculus verification

Perimeter

Objective and domainc(x)=2x+800xC{\left(x \right)} = 2 x + \frac{800}{x}
1≤x≤1001\le x\le 100
Differentiateⅆⅆxc(x)=2-800x2\frac{d}{d x} C{\left(x \right)} = 2 - \frac{800}{x^{2}}
Compare admissible candidates
xC
11802802
20208080
100100208208
Verified min: x=20x = 20. The interactive diagram and graph use this same objective.
Open persistent student workspace
QUESTION 5

Two Numbers

Question 5Level 1
Two positive numbers have sum 20. (a) Minimize the sum of their squares. (b) Maximize the product of one number and the square of the other.
Open interactive graph and model
Show complete solution

Let the numbers be x and 20 − x, where 0 < x < 20. Then S′(x) = 4x − 40 = 0 gives x = 10, and S′′(x) = 4 > 0.

Model and calculus verification

Sum of squares

Objective and domainf(x)=x2+(x-20)2F{\left(x \right)} = x^{2} + \left(x - 20\right)^{2}
0≤x≤200\le x\le 20
Differentiateⅆⅆxf(x)=4x-40\frac{d}{d x} F{\left(x \right)} = 4 x - 40
Compare admissible candidates
xF
00400400
1010200200
2020400400
Verified min: x=10x = 10. The interactive diagram and graph use this same objective.

Product

Objective and domainf(x)=x2(20-x)F{\left(x \right)} = x^{2} \left(20 - x\right)
0≤x≤200\le x\le 20
Differentiateⅆⅆxf(x)=-x(3x-40)\frac{d}{d x} F{\left(x \right)} = - x \left(3 x - 40\right)
Compare admissible candidates
xF
0000
13.333313.33331185.191185.19
202000
Verified max: x=13.3333x = 13.3333. The interactive diagram and graph use this same objective.
Open persistent student workspace
QUESTION 6

Three-Sided River Enclosure

Question 6Level 1
A 245000 m² rectangular enclosure borders a river, so only three sides need fencing. Find dimensions minimizing fence.
Open interactive graph and model
Show complete solution

700 m along the river and 350 m perpendicular to it; minimum fence 1400 m.

Model and calculus verification

Fence length

Objective and domainc(x)=2x+245000xC{\left(x \right)} = 2 x + \frac{245000}{x}
1≤x≤10001\le x\le 1000
Differentiateⅆⅆxc(x)=2-245000x2\frac{d}{d x} C{\left(x \right)} = 2 - \frac{245000}{x^{2}}
Compare admissible candidates
xC
11245002245002
35035014001400
1000100022452245
Verified min: x=350x = 350. The interactive diagram and graph use this same objective.
Open persistent student workspace
QUESTION 7

Rectangle Under a Parabola

Question 7Level 1
Find the maximum area of a rectangle above the x-axis whose upper vertices lie on y=12−x2.
Open interactive graph and model
Show complete solution

By symmetry, place the upper vertices at (±x, 12 − x2), where 0 < x < √ 12. The width is 2x and the height is 12 − x2.

Model and calculus verification

Area

Objective and domaina(x)=2x(12-x2)A{\left(x \right)} = 2 x \left(12 - x^{2}\right)
0≤x≤3.46410\le x\le 3.4641
Differentiateⅆⅆxa(x)=24-6x2\frac{d}{d x} A{\left(x \right)} = 24 - 6 x^{2}
Compare admissible candidates
xA
0000
223232
232 \sqrt{3}00
Verified max: x=2x = 2. The interactive diagram and graph use this same objective.
Open persistent student workspace
QUESTION 8

Open Box of Maximum Volume

Question 8Level 1
Cut equal squares from the corners of an 18 in × 18 in sheet and fold an open box. Find the maximum-volume box.
Open interactive graph and model
Show complete solution

V ′ ( x ) = 4 ( 9 − x ) ( 9 − 3 x ) Candidates are x=0,3,9. The maximum is V(3)=432 in³, giving dimensions 12 in × 12 in × 3 in.

Model and calculus verification

Volume

Objective and domainv(x)=4x(x-9)2V{\left(x \right)} = 4 x \left(x - 9\right)^{2}
0≤x≤90\le x\le 9
Differentiateⅆⅆxv(x)=12(x-9)(x-3)\frac{d}{d x} V{\left(x \right)} = 12 \left(x - 9\right) \left(x - 3\right)
Compare admissible candidates
xV
0000
33432432
9900
Verified max: x=3x = 3. The interactive diagram and graph use this same objective.
Open persistent student workspace
QUESTION 9

Three-Section Field

Question 9Level 1
A farmer has 1000 m of fencing for a rectangle divided into three sections by two internal fences parallel to one side. Find the maximum possible area.
Open interactive graph and model
Show complete solution

Let the side parallel to each internal fence be y, and let the other side be x. The total fencing is 2x + 4y = 1000 =⇒ x = 500 − 2y.

Model and calculus verification

Area

Objective and domaina(W)=2W(250-W)A{\left(W \right)} = 2 W \left(250 - W\right)
0≤W≤2500\le W\le 250
DifferentiateⅆⅆWa(W)=500-4W\frac{d}{d W} A{\left(W \right)} = 500 - 4 W
Compare admissible candidates
WA
0000
1251253125031250
25025000
Verified max: W=125W = 125. The interactive diagram and graph use this same objective.
Open persistent student workspace
QUESTION 10

Asymmetric Curve Rectangle

Question 10Level 1
A rectangle has its base on the x-axis. Its upper-right vertex lies on y=12−x24 and its upper-left vertex lies on y=12−x2. Find the largest possible area.
Open interactive graph and model
Show complete solution

Let the upper-right vertex have x-coordinate a > 0, and let the upper-left vertex have coordinate −b, where b > 0. Equal heights give 12 − a2 4 = 12 − b2 =⇒ b = a 2 .

Model and calculus verification

Area

Objective and domaina(a)=3a(48-a2)8A{\left(a \right)} = \frac{3 a \left(48 - a^{2}\right)}{8}
0≤a≤6.92820\le a\le 6.9282
Differentiateⅆⅆaa(a)=18-9a28\frac{d}{d a} A{\left(a \right)} = 18 - \frac{9 a^{2}}{8}
Compare admissible candidates
aA
0000
444848
434 \sqrt{3}00
Verified max: a=4a = 4. The interactive diagram and graph use this same objective.
Open persistent student workspace
QUESTION 11

Triangle Under a Parabola

Question 11Level 1
An isosceles triangle has its vertex at the origin and its other two vertices on y=27−x2. Find its maximum possible area.
Open interactive graph and model
Show complete solution

The two upper vertices are (±x, 27 − x2). The base is 2x and the perpendicular height from the origin is 27 − x2.

Model and calculus verification

Area

Objective and domaina(x)=x(27-x2)A{\left(x \right)} = x \left(27 - x^{2}\right)
0≤x≤5.19620\le x\le 5.1962
Differentiateⅆⅆxa(x)=27-3x2\frac{d}{d x} A{\left(x \right)} = 27 - 3 x^{2}
Compare admissible candidates
xA
0000
335454
333 \sqrt{3}00
Verified max: x=3x = 3. The interactive diagram and graph use this same objective.
Open persistent student workspace
QUESTION 12

Rectangle in a Triangle

Question 12Level 1
An isosceles triangle has base 8 cm and height 10 cm. A rectangle is placed inside it with two vertices on the base and two on the equal sides. Find the maximizing rectangle.
Open interactive graph and model
Show complete solution

Let the rectangle height be y. By similar triangles, the available width at height y is w = 8 ( 1 − y 10 ) .

Model and calculus verification

Area

Objective and domaina(y)=4y(10-y)5A{\left(y \right)} = \frac{4 y \left(10 - y\right)}{5}
0≤y≤100\le y\le 10
Differentiateⅆⅆya(y)=8-8y5\frac{d}{d y} A{\left(y \right)} = 8 - \frac{8 y}{5}
Compare admissible candidates
yA
0000
552020
101000
Verified max: y=5y = 5. The interactive diagram and graph use this same objective.
Open persistent student workspace
QUESTION 13

Trapezoid Under a Parabola

Question 13Level 1
A trapezoid lies under y=9−x2 and above the x-axis. Its lower base joins the x-intercepts and its upper vertices are symmetric points on the parabola. Maximize its area.
Open interactive graph and model
Show complete solution

The lower base has length 6. Let the upper vertices be (±x, 9 − x2), so the upper base has length 2x and the height is 9 − x2.

Model and calculus verification

Area

Objective and domaina(x)=-(x+3)(x2-9)A{\left(x \right)} = - \left(x + 3\right) \left(x^{2} - 9\right)
0≤x≤30\le x\le 3
Differentiateⅆⅆxa(x)=-3x2-6x+9\frac{d}{d x} A{\left(x \right)} = - 3 x^{2} - 6 x + 9
Compare admissible candidates
xA
002727
113232
3300
Verified max: x=1x = 1. The interactive diagram and graph use this same objective.
Open persistent student workspace
QUESTION 14

Minimum-Area Intercept Line

Question 14Level 1
A point A(a,b) lies in the first quadrant. Find the line through A that intersects the positive coordinate axes and forms a triangle of minimum area.
Open interactive graph and model
Show complete solution

Let the intercepts be p > a and q > b. The line has equation x/p + y/q = 1, and because (a, b) lies on it, a p + b q = 1 =⇒ q = bp p − a .

Model and calculus verification

Triangle area

Objective and domaina(p)=3p22p-4A{\left(p \right)} = \frac{3 p^{2}}{2 \left(p - 2\right)}
2.05≤p≤122.05\le p\le 12
Differentiateⅆⅆpa(p)=3p(p-4)2(p-2)2\frac{d}{d p} A{\left(p \right)} = \frac{3 p \left(p - 4\right)}{2 \left(p - 2\right)^{2}}
Compare admissible candidates
pA
2.052.05126.075126.075
441212
121221.621.6
Verified min: p=4p = 4. The interactive diagram and graph use this same objective.

Interactive instance a=2, b=3; the general ratio is p=2a.

Open persistent student workspace
QUESTION 15

Intercept Line Through (2,3)

Question 15Level 1
Find the line through (2,3) that cuts from the first quadrant a triangle of minimum area.
Open interactive graph and model
Show complete solution

Apply Problem 14 with a = 2 and b = 3. The optimal intercepts are 4 and 6.

Model and calculus verification

Triangle area

Objective and domaina(p)=3p22p-4A{\left(p \right)} = \frac{3 p^{2}}{2 \left(p - 2\right)}
2.05≤p≤122.05\le p\le 12
Differentiateⅆⅆpa(p)=3p(p-4)2(p-2)2\frac{d}{d p} A{\left(p \right)} = \frac{3 p \left(p - 4\right)}{2 \left(p - 2\right)^{2}}
Compare admissible candidates
pA
2.052.05126.075126.075
441212
121221.621.6
Verified min: p=4p = 4. The interactive diagram and graph use this same objective.

QUESTION 16

Parallel Segment in a Triangle

Question 16Level 1
A triangle has base 12 cm and altitude 16 cm. A segment parallel to the base and a point on the original base form a smaller triangle. Find the perpendicular distance y that maximizes its area.
Open interactive graph and model
Show complete solution

The small top triangle is similar to the original. If the parallel segment has length w, then w 12 = 16 − y 16 , w = 3 4 (16 − y).

Model and calculus verification

Area

Objective and domaina(y)=3y(16-y)8A{\left(y \right)} = \frac{3 y \left(16 - y\right)}{8}
0≤y≤160\le y\le 16
Differentiateⅆⅆya(y)=6-3y4\frac{d}{d y} A{\left(y \right)} = 6 - \frac{3 y}{4}
Compare admissible candidates
yA
0000
882424
161600
Verified max: y=8y = 8. The interactive diagram and graph use this same objective.
Open persistent student workspace
QUESTION 17

Isosceles Trapezoid

Question 17Level 1
An isosceles trapezoid has three sides of length 20 cm. Find the fourth side that maximizes area.
Open interactive graph and model
Show complete solution

Take the upper base and the two equal legs to be 20, and let the fourth side be b. Then the trapezoid height is √ 202 − u2, and b = 20 + 2u.

Model and calculus verification

Trapezoid area

Objective and domaina(u)=400-u2(u+20)A{\left(u \right)} = \sqrt{400 - u^{2}} \left(u + 20\right)
0≤u≤200\le u\le 20
Differentiateⅆⅆua(u)=-u2-u(u+20)+400400-u2\frac{d}{d u} A{\left(u \right)} = \frac{- u^{2} - u \left(u + 20\right) + 400}{\sqrt{400 - u^{2}}}
Compare admissible candidates
uA
00400400
1010519.615519.615
202000
Verified max: u=10u = 10. The interactive diagram and graph use this same objective.
Open persistent student workspace
QUESTION 18

Stadium Field

Question 18Level 1
A stadium-shaped field consists of a rectangle capped by two semicircles. Straight fencing costs 4 QAR/m and curved fencing 6 QAR/m. Total cost is 400 QAR. Maximize area.
Open interactive graph and model
Show complete solution

Let each straight side have length x and let the semicircle radius be r. The two curved ends together form one full circle.

Model and calculus verification

Area

Objective and domaina(r)=2r(-πr+50)A{\left(r \right)} = 2 r \left(- \pi r + 50\right)
0≤r≤10.610\le r\le 10.61
Differentiateⅆⅆra(r)=-4πr+100\frac{d}{d r} A{\left(r \right)} = - 4 \pi r + 100
Compare admissible candidates
rA
0000
7.957757.95775397.887397.887
10.610310.6103353.678353.678
Verified max: r=7.95775r = 7.95775. The interactive diagram and graph use this same objective.

QUESTION 19

Maximum Box from Fixed Cost

Question 19Level 1
An open-top square-based box is made from wood costing 3 QAR/m². The total manufacturing cost is 144 QAR. Find the largest possible volume.
Open interactive graph and model
Show complete solution

The available wood area is 144 3 = 48 m² . Let the square base side be x and the height be h.

Model and calculus verification

Volume

Objective and domainv(x)=x(48-x2)4V{\left(x \right)} = \frac{x \left(48 - x^{2}\right)}{4}
0≤x≤6.92820\le x\le 6.9282
Differentiateⅆⅆxv(x)=12-3x24\frac{d}{d x} V{\left(x \right)} = 12 - \frac{3 x^{2}}{4}
Compare admissible candidates
xV
0000
443232
434 \sqrt{3}00
Verified max: x=4x = 4. The interactive diagram and graph use this same objective.

QUESTION 20

Least-Material Open Box

Question 20Level 1
An open-top box has square base and volume 32 cm³. Find its dimensions using the least material.
Open interactive graph and model
Show complete solution

Let the square base side be x and height be h. The material area is S(x) = x2 + 4xh = x2 + 128 x .

Model and calculus verification

Material area

Objective and domaina(x)=x3+128xA{\left(x \right)} = \frac{x^{3} + 128}{x}
0.5≤x≤120.5\le x\le 12
Differentiateⅆⅆxa(x)=2x-128x2\frac{d}{d x} A{\left(x \right)} = 2 x - \frac{128}{x^{2}}
Compare admissible candidates
xA
12\frac{1}{2}256.25256.25
444848
1212154.667154.667
Verified min: x=4x = 4. The interactive diagram and graph use this same objective.
Open persistent student workspace
QUESTION 21

Light-Transmission Window

Question 21Level 1
A window is a rectangle topped by a semicircle. The semicircle admits half as much light per m² as the rectangle. The outer perimeter is 6 m. Find dimensions maximizing effective light.
Open interactive graph and model
Show complete solution

Let the semicircle radius be r and rectangular height be h. The perimeter is 2h + 2r + πr = 6 =⇒ h = 6 − (2 + π)r 2 .

Model and calculus verification

Effective light

Objective and domainf(r)=r(-r(8+3π)+24)4F{\left(r \right)} = \frac{r \left(- r \left(8 + 3 \pi\right) + 24\right)}{4}
0≤r≤1.1670\le r\le 1.167
Differentiateⅆⅆrf(r)=-3πr2-4r+6\frac{d}{d r} F{\left(r \right)} = - \frac{3 \pi r}{2} - 4 r + 6
Compare admissible candidates
rF
0000
0.6886740.6886742.066022.06602
1.166951.166951.069541.06954
Verified max: r=0.688674r = 0.688674. The interactive diagram and graph use this same objective.
Open persistent student workspace
QUESTION 22

Advertisement Margins

Question 22Level 1
A rectangular sheet has area 50 cm². An advertisement has 1 cm top/bottom margins and 0.5 cm side margins. Find the sheet dimensions maximizing printed area.
Open interactive graph and model
Show complete solution

Let the sheet width be x and height be y. The printed dimensions are x − 1 and y − 2, so A(x) = (x − 1) ( 50 x − 2 ) = 52 − 2x − 50 x .

Model and calculus verification

Printed area

Objective and domaina(x)=-2x+52-50xA{\left(x \right)} = - 2 x + 52 - \frac{50}{x}
1.01≤x≤251.01\le x\le 25
Differentiateⅆⅆxa(x)=-2+50x2\frac{d}{d x} A{\left(x \right)} = -2 + \frac{50}{x^{2}}
Compare admissible candidates
xA
1.011.010.4750490.475049
553232
252500
Verified max: x=5x = 5. The interactive diagram and graph use this same objective.
Open persistent student workspace
QUESTION 23

Folded Corner

Question 23Level 1
A rectangle has height 3 cm. Its upper-left corner is folded to a point on the lower side. Find the maximum area of the right triangle formed by the crease geometry.
Open interactive graph and model
Show complete solution

Because the upper-left corner is folded to E, the crease point W is equidistant from those two points. Now WA = 3 − h and WE = √ x2 + h2, so (3 − h)2 = x2 + h2 =⇒ h = 9 − x2 6 .

Model and calculus verification

Triangle area

Objective and domaina(x)=x(9-x2)12A{\left(x \right)} = \frac{x \left(9 - x^{2}\right)}{12}
0≤x≤30\le x\le 3
Differentiateⅆⅆxa(x)=-x2-34\frac{d}{d x} A{\left(x \right)} = - \frac{x^{2} - 3}{4}
Compare admissible candidates
xA
0000
3\sqrt{3}32\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}
3300
Verified max: x=3x = \sqrt{3}. The interactive diagram and graph use this same objective.
Open persistent student workspace
QUESTION 24

Two Trains

Question 24Level 1
At 10:00 a.m. one train leaves south at 60 km/h. Another travels west at 45 km/h and reaches the first station at 11:00 a.m. When are they closest?
Open interactive graph and model
Show complete solution

At t=0.36 h after 10:00, approximately 10:21:36 a.m.

Model and calculus verification

Distance²

Objective and domaind(t)=5625t2-4050t+2025D{\left(t \right)} = 5625 t^{2} - 4050 t + 2025
0≤t≤10\le t\le 1
Differentiateⅆⅆtd(t)=11250t-4050\frac{d}{d t} D{\left(t \right)} = 11250 t - 4050
Compare admissible candidates
tD
0020252025
0.360.3612961296
1136003600
Verified min: t=0.36t = 0.36. The interactive diagram and graph use this same objective.

Find the point on y=8x closest to (4,2).
Open interactive graph and model
Show complete solution

(2,4).

QUESTION 26

Closest Points on a Parabola

Question 26Level 1
Find the point(s) on y=4−x2 closest to (0,2).
Open interactive graph and model
Show complete solution

( d 2 ) ′ = 2 x ( 2 x 2 − 3 ) The critical values are x=0 and x=±√(3/2). The two absolute minima occur at (±√(3/2),5/2).

Model and calculus verification

Distance²

Objective and domaind(x)=x2+(x2-2)2D{\left(x \right)} = x^{2} + \left(x^{2} - 2\right)^{2}
-2≤x≤2-2\le x\le 2
Differentiateⅆⅆxd(x)=4x3-6x\frac{d}{d x} D{\left(x \right)} = 4 x^{3} - 6 x
Compare admissible candidates
xD
-2-288
-62- \frac{\sqrt{6}}{2}74\frac{7}{4}
0044
62\frac{\sqrt{6}}{2}74\frac{7}{4}
2288
Verified min: x=-62x = - \frac{\sqrt{6}}{2}; x=62x = \frac{\sqrt{6}}{2}. The interactive diagram and graph use this same objective.

QUESTION 27

Closest Point on a Radical Curve

Question 27Level 1
Find the point on y=8x closest to (4,2).
Open interactive graph and model
Show complete solution

A point on the curve is (x, √ 8x), with x ≥ 0. Minimize the squared distance D2 (x) = (x − 4)2 + ( √ 8x − 2)2 .

Model and calculus verification

Distance²

Objective and domaind(x)=-82x+x2+20D{\left(x \right)} = - 8 \sqrt{2} \sqrt{x} + x^{2} + 20
0≤x≤80\le x\le 8
Differentiateⅆⅆxd(x)=2x-42x\frac{d}{d x} D{\left(x \right)} = 2 x - \frac{4 \sqrt{2}}{\sqrt{x}}
Compare admissible candidates
xD
002020
2288
885252
Verified min: x=2x = 2. The interactive diagram and graph use this same objective.
Open persistent student workspace
QUESTION 28

Moving Particles

Question 28Level 1
One particle starts at (0,8) and moves down the y-axis at 4 cm/s. Another starts at (10,0) and moves right at 2 cm/s. When is their distance least?
Open interactive graph and model
Show complete solution

After t seconds, the particles are P(t) = (0, 8 − 4t), Q(t) = (10 + 2t, 0). For 0 ≤ t ≤ 2, minimize D2 (t) = (10 + 2t)2 + (8 − 4t)2 .

Model and calculus verification

Distance²

Objective and domaind(t)=20t2-24t+164D{\left(t \right)} = 20 t^{2} - 24 t + 164
0≤t≤20\le t\le 2
Differentiateⅆⅆtd(t)=40t-24\frac{d}{d t} D{\left(t \right)} = 40 t - 24
Compare admissible candidates
tD
00164164
35\frac{3}{5}156.8156.8
22196196
Verified min: t=35t = \frac{3}{5}. The interactive diagram and graph use this same objective.

QUESTION 29

Cylinder in a Cone

Question 29Level 1
A right circular cylinder is inscribed coaxially in a cone of base radius 5 cm and height 9 cm. Find the cylinder dimensions that maximize volume.
Open interactive graph and model
Show complete solution

Let the cylinder height be h. Similar triangles give r 5 = 9 − h 9 , r = 5 ( 1 − h 9 ) .

Model and calculus verification

Cylinder volume

Objective and domainv(h)=25πh(h-9)281V{\left(h \right)} = \frac{25 \pi h \left(h - 9\right)^{2}}{81}
0≤h≤90\le h\le 9
Differentiateⅆⅆhv(h)=25π(h-9)(h-3)27\frac{d}{d h} V{\left(h \right)} = \frac{25 \pi \left(h - 9\right) \left(h - 3\right)}{27}
Compare admissible candidates
hV
0000
33104.72104.72
9900
Verified max: h=3h = 3. The interactive diagram and graph use this same objective.

QUESTION 30

Cone in a Sphere

Question 30Level 1
A right circular cone is inscribed in a sphere of radius 9 cm. Find the cone's base radius for maximum volume.
Open interactive graph and model
Show complete solution

Let h be the cone height measured from the sphere’s top point to the base plane. In an axial cross-section, r2 = 92 − (9 − h)2 = 18h − h2 .

Model and calculus verification

Cone volume

Objective and domainv(h)=πh2(18-h)3V{\left(h \right)} = \frac{\pi h^{2} \left(18 - h\right)}{3}
0≤h≤180\le h\le 18
Differentiateⅆⅆhv(h)=-πh(h-12)\frac{d}{d h} V{\left(h \right)} = - \pi h \left(h - 12\right)
Compare admissible candidates
hV
0000
1212904.779904.779
181800
Verified max: h=12h = 12. The interactive diagram and graph use this same objective.

QUESTION 31

Cylinder in a Frustum

Question 31Level 1
A frustum has radii 14 cm and 5 cm and height 12 cm. A coaxial cylinder rests on the larger base and touches the lateral surface. Maximize the cylinder's volume.
Open interactive graph and model
Show complete solution

Let h be the cylinder height. The frustum radius decreases linearly from 14 to 5, so at height h, r(h) = 14 − 14 − 5 12 h = 14 − 3 4 h.

Model and calculus verification

Cylinder volume

Objective and domainv(h)=πh(3h-56)216V{\left(h \right)} = \frac{\pi h \left(3 h - 56\right)^{2}}{16}

Prism in a Sphere

Question 32Level 1
A square-based rectangular prism is inscribed in a sphere of radius 10 cm. Find the dimensions maximizing volume.
Open interactive graph and model
Show complete solution

Hollowed Cylinder

Question 34Level 1
A cylindrical piece of wood has lateral area 400π cm². A hemisphere whose diameter equals the cylinder's base diameter is hollowed out. Find the radius maximizing the remaining volume.
Open interactive graph and model
Show complete solution

Let the cylinder radius be r and height be h. The lateral area condition gives 2πrh = 400π =⇒ h = 200 r .

Remaining volume

QUESTION 35

Rolled Sheet Cylinder

Question 35Level 1
A rectangular sheet has perimeter 36 cm. It is rolled so that length x becomes circumference and width y becomes height. Find x and y for maximum cylinder volume.
Open interactive graph and model
Show complete solution

The perimeter condition gives x + y = 18, so y = 18 − x. Since x = 2πr, we have r = x/(2π).

Cylinder volume

Sector Rolled to Cone

Question 38Level 1
A circular sector has radius L and angle θ radians. It is rolled into a cone, with L becoming slant height. Find θ that maximizes cone volume.
Open interactive graph and model
Show complete solution

The sector arc becomes the base circumference: Lθ = 2πr =⇒ r = Lθ 2π . The cone height is h = √ L2 − r2, so V = π 3 r2 p L2 − r2.

Isosceles Sheet Solid

Question 40Level 1
An isosceles triangular sheet has perimeter 40 cm and is revolved about its base. Find the maximum volume of the resulting solid.
Open interactive graph and model
Show complete solution

Let the base be b and each equal side be ℓ = (40 − b)/2. The altitude is the radius of the solid: r2 = ℓ2 − ( b 2 )2 = ( 20 − b 2 )2 − b2 4 = 400 − 20b.

Cylinder with Fixed Surface Area

Question 42Level 1
A closed cylinder is made from metal of total area 600π cm². Find the largest possible volume.
Open interactive graph and model

QUESTION 45

Right Triangle in a Circle

Question 45Level 1
A circle has diameter AB=4 cm. Point C moves on the circle. Find ∠ABC when right triangle ACB has maximum area.
Open interactive graph and model
Show complete solution

Since AB is the hypotenuse, AC = 4 sin θ, BC = 4 cos θ. Thus A(θ) = 1 2 (4 sin θ)(4 cos θ) = 4 sin(2θ).

Area

Variable-Angle Quadrilateral

Question 46Level 1
In quadrilateral MBJD, MB=2 cm, MD=1 cm, and ∠BMD=x. Triangle BJD is right isosceles at J. Find x maximizing the quadrilateral's area.
Open interactive graph and model
Show complete solution

The quadrilateral area is the sum of triangles MBD and BJD. By the cosine rule, BD2 = 22 + 12 − 2(2)(1) cos x = 5 − 4 cos x.

QUESTION 51

Box from a Net

Question 51Level 2
A box net is cut from a 30 cm × 16 cm sheet. If its height is x, the other dimensions are 15−x and 16−2x. Find the largest possible volume.
Open interactive graph and model
Show complete solution

The volume is V (x) = x(15 − x)(16 − 2x), with the physical restriction 0 < x < 8. Expanding and differentiating, V ′ (x) = 240 − 92x + 6x2 = 2(3x − 10)(x − 12).

Model and calculus verification

Volume

Objective and domainv(x)=2x(x-15)(x-8)V{\left(x \right)} = 2 x \left(x - 15\right) \left(x - 8\right)
0≤x≤80\le x\le 8

Compare admissible candidates
xV

3.333333.33333362.963362.963

Verified max: x=3.33333x = 3.33333. The interactive diagram and graph use this same objective.
Open persistent student workspace
QUESTION 52

Norman Window

Question 52Level 2
A rectangle topped by a semicircle uses 8+π miles of trim. Explain why area matters and find maximizing dimensions.
Open interactive graph and model
Show complete solution

Let r be the semicircle radius, so the window width is 2r and the rectangular height is h. The trim condition is 2h+2r+πr=8+π, so h=[8+π−(2+π)r]/2. The area is A(r)=2rh+(π/2)r²=(8+π)r−(2+π/2)r². Thus A′(r)=8+π−(4+π)r. Therefore r=(8+π)/(4+π)≈1.56010. The rectangular height is h≈1.56010, and the width is 2r≈3.12020. Since A is a downward-opening quadratic, this critical point gives the absolute maximum. Maximum-area dimensions: width ≈3.1202, rectangular height ≈1.5601, semicircle radius ≈1.5601.

Model and calculus verification

Window area

Objective and domaina(r)=r(-r(π+4)+2π+16)2A{\left(r \right)} = \frac{r \left(- r \left(\pi + 4\right) + 2 \pi + 16\right)}{2}
0≤r≤2.1670\le r\le 2.167
Differentiateⅆⅆra(r)=-4r-πr+π+8\frac{d}{d r} A{\left(r \right)} = - 4 r - \pi r + \pi + 8
Compare admissible candidates
rA
0000
1.56011.56018.690998.69099
2.166952.166957.375977.37597
Verified max: r=1.5601r = 1.5601. The interactive diagram and graph use this same objective.
Open persistent student workspace
QUESTION 53

Closest Point to a Parabola

Question 53Level 2
Find the point on y=9−x2 closest to (3,9), then compare with the point closest to (5,11).
Open interactive graph and model
Show complete solution

For (3,9), minimize f ( x ) = ( x − 3 ) 2 + x 4 . Then f ′ ( x ) = 2 ( x − 1 ) ( 2 x 2 + 2 x + 3 ) , so x=1 and the point is (1,8), distance √5. For (5,11), minimize f ( x ) = ( x − 5 ) 2 + ( x 2 + 2 ) 2 . The critical equation 4 x 3 + 10 x − 10 = 0 has x≈0.79728, giving point (0.79728,8.364) and distance ≈4.96.

Model and calculus verification

Distance² to (3,9)

Objective and domaind(x)=x4+(x-3)2D{\left(x \right)} = x^{4} + \left(x - 3\right)^{2}
-2.5≤x≤2.5-2.5\le x\le 2.5
Differentiateⅆⅆxd(x)=4x3+2x-6\frac{d}{d x} D{\left(x \right)} = 4 x^{3} + 2 x - 6
Compare admissible candidates
xD
-52- \frac{5}{2}69.312569.3125
1155
52\frac{5}{2}39.312539.3125
Verified min: x=1x = 1. The interactive diagram and graph use this same objective.

Distance² to (5,11)

QUESTION 54

Closest Distance Between Two Runners

Question 54Level 2
Two homes are 20 km apart north-south. One runner travels south at 8 km/h; the other travels east at 6 km/h. Both run for 2.5 h. When are they closest?
Open interactive graph and model
Show complete solution

s′(t)=0 at t=1.6 h. Comparing t=0,1.6,2.5 gives distances 20,12,15 km. Starting at 9:00 a.m., they are closest at 10:36 a.m.

Model and calculus verification

Distance

Objective and domaind(t)=225t2-80t+100D{\left(t \right)} = 2 \sqrt{25 t^{2} - 80 t + 100}
0≤t≤2.50\le t\le 2.5
Differentiateⅆⅆtd(t)=25(5t-8)5t2-16t+20\frac{d}{d t} D{\left(t \right)} = \frac{2 \sqrt{5} \left(5 t - 8\right)}{\sqrt{5 t^{2} - 16 t + 20}}
Compare admissible candidates
tD
002020
85\frac{8}{5}1212
52\frac{5}{2}1515
Verified min: t=85t = \frac{8}{5}. The interactive diagram and graph use this same objective.

QUESTION 55

Minimum Wire Joining Two Poles

Question 55Level 2
Poles of height 4 m and 8 m are 9 m apart. A ground stake between them is joined to both tops. Find the stake position minimizing total wire.
Open interactive graph and model
Show complete solution

The derivative equation gives x=3 m (the other algebraic root is outside the domain). Endpoint comparison gives W(3)=15 m, the absolute minimum.

Model and calculus verification

Total wire

Objective and domainf(x)=x2+16+(x-9)2+64F{\left(x \right)} = \sqrt{x^{2} + 16} + \sqrt{\left(x - 9\right)^{2} + 64}
0≤x≤90\le x\le 9
Differentiateⅆⅆxf(x)=xx2+16+xx2-18x+145-9x2+16x2+16x2-18x+145\frac{d}{d x} F{\left(x \right)} = \frac{x \sqrt{x^{2} + 16} + x \sqrt{x^{2} - 18 x + 145} - 9 \sqrt{x^{2} + 16}}{\sqrt{x^{2} + 16} \sqrt{x^{2} - 18 x + 145}}
Compare admissible candidates
xF
0016.041616.0416
331515
9917.848917.8489
Verified min: x=3x = 3. The interactive diagram and graph use this same objective.

QUESTION 56

Soda Can with Minimum Material

Question 56Level 2
A cylindrical can must hold 12 fl oz ≈ 21.65628 in³. Assuming uniform thickness, find dimensions minimizing surface area.
Open interactive graph and model
Show complete solution

A ′ ( r ) = 4 π r − 2 V r 2 = 0 ⇒ r 3 = V 2 π r≈1.51055 in and h≈3.0211 in. Thus h=2r: the optimal can is 'square' in axial cross-section.

Model and calculus verification

Surface area

Objective and domaina(r)=2πr3+43.31256rA{\left(r \right)} = \frac{2 \pi r^{3} + 43.31256}{r}
0.35≤r≤40.35\le r\le 4
Differentiateⅆⅆra(r)=4πr-43.31256r2\frac{d}{d r} A{\left(r \right)} = 4 \pi r - \frac{43.31256}{r^{2}}
Compare admissible candidates
rA
720\frac{7}{20}124.52124.52
1.510551.5105543.010143.0101
44111.359111.359
Verified min: r=1.51055r = 1.51055. The interactive diagram and graph use this same objective.

QUESTION 57

Cost, Revenue, Profit, and Marginal Functions

Question 57Level 2
A theatre has 1000 customers at 26 QAR. Each 1-QAR discount brings 50 extra customers, and each customer spends 4 QAR inside. Find the ticket price maximizing total revenue.
Open interactive graph and model
Show complete solution

R ( d ) = ( 1000 + 50 d ) ( 30 − d ) = − 50 d 2 + 500 d + 30000 R′=−100d+500=0 gives d=5. The optimal ticket price is 21 QAR.

Model and calculus verification

Total revenue

Objective and domainr(d)=-50(d-30)(d+20)R{\left(d \right)} = - 50 \left(d - 30\right) \left(d + 20\right)
0≤d≤200\le d\le 20
Differentiateⅆⅆdr(d)=500-100d\frac{d}{d d} R{\left(d \right)} = 500 - 100 d
Compare admissible candidates
dR
003000030000
553125031250
20202000020000
Verified max: d=5d = 5. The interactive diagram and graph use this same objective.
Open persistent student workspace
QUESTION 58

Maximum Viewing Angle

Question 58Level 2
A painting is 4 ft high and its lower edge is 1 ft above the camera lens. Find the camera distance maximizing the viewing angle.
Open interactive graph and model
Show complete solution

Maximize tan θ = 4 x x 2 + 5 . Its derivative changes sign at x = 5 , so the optimal distance is √5 ft.

Model and calculus verification

Viewing angle

Objective and domaing(x)=-arctan(1x)+arctan(5x)G{\left(x \right)} = - \operatorname{atan}{\left(\frac{1}{x} \right)} + \operatorname{atan}{\left(\frac{5}{x} \right)}
0.1≤x≤120.1\le x\le 12
Differentiateⅆⅆxg(x)=-4x2-20(x2+1)(x2+25)\frac{d}{d x} G{\left(x \right)} = - \frac{4 \left(x^{2} - 5\right)}{\left(x^{2} + 1\right) \left(x^{2} + 25\right)}
Compare admissible candidates
xG
0.10.10.07967130.0796713
5\sqrt{5}0.7297280.729728
12120.311650.31165
Verified max: x=5x = \sqrt{5}. The interactive diagram and graph use this same objective.

Show complete solution

Question 60Level 2

QUESTION 63

Central Polygon

Question 63Level 2
A 10 cm × 8 cm rectangle contains a point H on the base, 6 cm from the right corner. Points L and M lie on the vertical sides with ∠LHM=90°. Find x and y maximizing the central polygon's area.
Open interactive graph and model
Show complete solution

The two right triangles adjacent to H are similar because their acute angles are complementary. Their horizontal legs are 6 and 4, so x 6 = 4 y =⇒ xy = 24, y = 24 x .

Wire Cut into Two Squares

Question 65Level 2
A 2-ft wire is cut into two pieces, each forming a square. Choose the cut maximizing total area.
Open interactive graph and model
Show complete solution

The total area is a convex quadratic, so its maximum on the closed interval occurs at an endpoint: use all wire for one square.

Total area

QUESTION 66

Lidded Box from a 24×9 Sheet

Question 66Level 2
A 24 cm × 9 cm cardboard sheet has two congruent squares and two congruent rectangles removed as shown so it can be folded into a box with a lid. (1) Write V(x). (2) State its domain. (3) Find the dimensions maximizing volume.
Open interactive graph and model
Show complete solution

The box height is x, while the base dimensions are 12 − x and 9 − 2 x . Thus V ( x ) = x ( 12 − x ) ( 9 − 2 x ) , with 0 < x < 9 / 2 . Since V ′ ( x ) = 6 ( x − 2 ) ( x − 9 ) , the admissible critical value is x=2 cm. The maximum-volume box has base 10 cm × 5 cm, height 2 cm, and volume 100 cm³.

Model and calculus verification

Volume

Objective and domainv(x)=x(x-12)(2x-9)V{\left(x \right)} = x \left(x - 12\right) \left(2 x - 9\right)
0≤x≤4.50\le x\le 4.5
Differentiateⅆⅆxv(x)=6x2-66x+108\frac{d}{d x} V{\left(x \right)} = 6 x^{2} - 66 x + 108
Compare admissible candidates
xV
0000
22100100
4.54.500
Verified max: x=2x = 2. The interactive diagram and graph use this same objective.
Open persistent student workspace
QUESTION 67

Open Box: 12×16 Sheet

Question 67Level 2
Cut x-inch squares from a 12 in × 16 in sheet and fold an open box. Find x maximizing volume.
Open interactive graph and model
Show complete solution

The physical domain is 0<x<6. The volume is V(x)=x(12−2x)(16−2x). Then V′(x)=12x²−112x+192=4(3x²−28x+48). The roots are x=(14±2√13)/3, and only x=(14−2√13)/3≈2.2630 is admissible. The derivative changes from positive to negative at that value, and the endpoint volumes are 0, so it is the absolute maximizer.

Model and calculus verification

Volume

Objective and domainv(x)=4x(x-8)(x-6)V{\left(x \right)} = 4 x \left(x - 8\right) \left(x - 6\right)
0≤x≤60\le x\le 6
Differentiateⅆⅆxv(x)=4(3x2-28x+48)\frac{d}{d x} V{\left(x \right)} = 4 \left(3 x^{2} - 28 x + 48\right)
Compare admissible candidates
xV
0000
2.262972.26297194.067194.067
6600
Verified max: x=2.26297x = 2.26297. The interactive diagram and graph use this same objective.
Open persistent student workspace
QUESTION 68

Two Boxes from Cut Squares

Question 68Level 2
A 6 in×6 in6\text{ in}\times 6\text{ in} square sheet has xx-inch squares cut from its four corners. The remaining cross-shaped sheet is folded into an open box. The four removed squares are stacked to form a second square prism with base x×xx\times x and height x4\frac{x}{4}. Maximize the total volume of the two solids.
Open interactive graph and model
Show complete solution

The first box has volume V1(x)=x(6−2x)2V_1(x)=x(6-2x)^2. The second prism has volume V2(x)=x2(x4)=x34V_2(x)=x^2\left(\frac{x}{4}\right)=\frac{x^3}{4}. Therefore V(x)=x(6−2x)2+x34V(x)=x(6-2x)^2+\frac{x^3}{4} on x≥0∧x≤30\le x\le 3. The admissible interior maximizer is x=3217-4⁢1317x=\frac{32-4\sqrt{13}}{17}\approx1.034\text{ in}.

Model and calculus verification

Total volume

Objective and domainv(x)=x(x2+16(x-3)2)4V{\left(x \right)} = \frac{x \left(x^{2} + 16 \left(x - 3\right)^{2}\right)}{4}
0≤x≤30\le x\le 3
Differentiateⅆⅆxv(x)=51x24-48x+36\frac{d}{d x} V{\left(x \right)} = \frac{51 x^{2}}{4} - 48 x + 36
Compare admissible candidates
xV
0000
1.033991.0339916.262716.2627
2.730722.730725.882675.88267
336.756.75
Verified max: x=1.03399x = 1.03399. The interactive diagram and graph use this same objective.
Open persistent student workspace
QUESTION 69

Parameter d in Two-Box Problem

Question 69Level 2
For a d-in×4-ind\text{-in}\times4\text{-in} sheet, where d≥4d\ge4, squares of side xx are cut from the corners to form an open box. The four removed squares are stacked to form a second square prism with base x×xx\times x and height x4\frac{x}{4}. Determine, in terms of dd, when the maximizing design uses x>0x>0, so that both solids have positive volume.
Open interactive graph and model
Show complete solution

For d≥4d\ge4, the total volume is Vd(x)=x(d−2x)(4−2x)+x34V_d(x)=x(d-2x)(4-2x)+\frac{x^3}{4} on x≥0∧x≤20\le x\le2. Since Vd′(0)=4d>0V_d'(0)=4d>0, the endpoint x=0x=0 cannot maximize the volume. Hence, for every d≥4d\ge4, the maximizing design uses a positive cut and produces both solids. The model selector illustrates several values of dd.

Model and calculus verification

Total volume, d=4

Objective and domainv(x)=x(x2+16(x-2)2)4V{\left(x \right)} = \frac{x \left(x^{2} + 16 \left(x - 2\right)^{2}\right)}{4}
0≤x≤20\le x\le 2
Differentiateⅆⅆxv(x)=51x2-128x+644\frac{d}{d x} V{\left(x \right)} = \frac{51 x^{2} - 128 x + 64}{4}
Compare admissible candidates
xV
0000
0.6893250.6893254.818574.81857
1.820481.820481.743011.74301
2222
Verified max: x=0.689325x = 0.689325. The interactive diagram and graph use this same objective.

Illustrative member of the family with d=4. The maximizer is interior.

Objective and domainv(x)=x(17x2-80x+96)4V{\left(x \right)} = \frac{x \left(17 x^{2} - 80 x + 96\right)}{4}
0≤x≤20\le x\le 2
Differentiateⅆⅆxv(x)=51x2-160x+964\frac{d}{d x} V{\left(x \right)} = \frac{51 x^{2} - 160 x + 96}{4}

QUESTION 70

Two-Glass Window

Question 70Level 2
A rectangle of width x and height y is topped by a semicircle of diameter x. The top glass admits 1 light unit/m² and the rectangle 3 units/m². Ten metres of frame include the outer boundary and divider. Maximize transmitted light.
Open interactive graph and model
Show complete solution

Constraint: 2y+x(2+π/2)=10. Effective light L=3xy+πx²/8. The optimum is x=60/(24+5π), y=10(6+π)/(24+5π).

Transmitted light

QUESTION 71

Closest Point on an Ellipse

Question 71Level 2
Find the point(s) on 4x2+y2=4 closest to (0,1).
Open interactive graph and model
Show complete solution

From 4x²+y²=4, we have x²=1−y²/4. The squared distance to (0,1) is D²(y)=x²+(y−1)²=2+(3/4)y²−2y, −2≤y≤2. Thus (D²)′=(3/2)y−2, so y=4/3. Then x²=1−(16/9)/4=5/9, giving The closest points are (±√5/3, 4/3), and the minimum distance is 2/3.

Model and calculus verification

Distance²

Objective and domaind(y)=3y24-2y+2D{\left(y \right)} = \frac{3 y^{2}}{4} - 2 y + 2
-2≤y≤2-2\le y\le 2
Differentiateⅆⅆyd(y)=3y2-2\frac{d}{d y} D{\left(y \right)} = \frac{3 y}{2} - 2
Compare admissible candidates
yD
-2-299
43\frac{4}{3}23\frac{2}{3}
2211
Verified min: y=43y = \frac{4}{3}. The interactive diagram and graph use this same objective.
Open persistent student workspace
QUESTION 72

Row and Run Across a Circular Lake

Question 72Level 2
A man at A wants to reach the diametrically opposite point C on a circular lake of radius 3 km. He may row at 3 km/h from A to B and then run along the shore at 6 km/h from B to C. Determine B minimizing total time and justify.
Open interactive graph and model
Show complete solution

Let θ=∠BAC, 0≤θ≤π/2. Then AB=6 cosθ and arc BC=6θ, so T ( θ ) = 2 cos θ + θ . The interior critical point θ=π/6 is a maximum. Comparing endpoints gives T(0)=2 h and T(π/2)=π/2 h. The minimum occurs at B=A: he should run the entire semicircle and not row.

Model and calculus verification

Travel time

Objective and domaint(θ)=θ+2cos⁡(θ)T{\left(\theta \right)} = \theta + 2 \cos{\left(\theta \right)}
0≤θ≤1.57080\le \theta\le 1.5708
Differentiateⅆⅆθt(θ)=1-2sin⁡(θ)\frac{d}{d \theta} T{\left(\theta \right)} = 1 - 2 \sin{\left(\theta \right)}
Compare admissible candidates
θT
0022
π6\frac{\pi}{6}2.255652.25565
π2\frac{\pi}{2}π2\frac{\pi}{2}
Verified min: θ=π2\theta = \frac{\pi}{2}. The interactive diagram and graph use this same objective.

The interior critical point θ=π/6 is a maximum; the absolute minimum is an endpoint.

Open persistent student workspace

Closest Point on y=cos x to (1,1)

Question 73Level 2

Open interactive graph and model
Show complete solution

Minimize D 2 = ( x − 1 ) 2 + ( cos x − 1 ) 2 numerically on a justified interval.

Distance²

Objective and domaind(x)=(x-1)2+(cos⁡(x)-1)2D{\left(x \right)} = \left(x - 1\right)^{2} + \left(\cos{\left(x \right)} - 1\right)^{2}
-3≤x≤4-3\le x\le 4
Differentiateⅆⅆxd(x)=2x-2(cos⁡(x)-1)sin⁡(x)-2\frac{d}{d x} D{\left(x \right)} = 2 x - 2 \left(\cos{\left(x \right)} - 1\right) \sin{\left(x \right)} - 2
Compare admissible candidates
xD
-3-319.960119.9601
0.7897810.7897810.1318080.131808
4411.734511.7345
Verified min: x=0.789781x = 0.789781. The interactive diagram and graph use this same objective.
Open persistent student workspace
QUESTION 74

Bicycle Route and Equal-Angle Principle

Question 74Level 2
A cyclist travels from a house 55 km from a straight highway to a school 77 km from the highway. The perpendicular feet of the house and school are 99 km apart. Let xx be the distance along the highway from the foot nearest the house to the chosen joining point. (a) Write L(x)L(x). (b) Show that L′(x)=0L'(x)=0 implies sin(α)=sin(β)\sin\alpha=\sin\beta. (c) Find the value of xx that minimizes the total route length.
Open interactive graph and model
Show complete solution

The route length is L(x)=x2+25+(9−x)2+49L(x)=\sqrt{x^2+25}+\sqrt{(9-x)^2+49} for x≥0∧x≤90\le x\le9. The critical-point equation is xx2+25=9-x(9-x)2+49\frac{x}{\sqrt{x^2+25}}=\frac{9-x}{\sqrt{(9-x)^2+49}}. These ratios are the sines of the two route angles, so sin(α)=sin(β)\sin\alpha=\sin\beta. Solving gives x=154x=\frac{15}{4}\text{ km}.

Model and calculus verification

Route length

Objective and domainf(x)=x2+25+(x-9)2+49F{\left(x \right)} = \sqrt{x^{2} + 25} + \sqrt{\left(x - 9\right)^{2} + 49}
0≤x≤90\le x\le 9
Differentiateⅆⅆxf(x)=xx2+25+xx2-18x+130-9x2+25x2+25x2-18x+130\frac{d}{d x} F{\left(x \right)} = \frac{x \sqrt{x^{2} + 25} + x \sqrt{x^{2} - 18 x + 130} - 9 \sqrt{x^{2} + 25}}{\sqrt{x^{2} + 25} \sqrt{x^{2} - 18 x + 130}}
Compare admissible candidates
xF
0016.401816.4018
3.753.751515
9917.295617.2956
Verified min: x=3.75x = 3.75. The interactive diagram and graph use this same objective.
Open persistent student workspace
QUESTION 75

Shortest New Road Through a Fixed Point

Question 75Level 2
Two perpendicular roads meet outside a garden. In the diagram, P is 8 km from the first road and 1 km from the second road. A new straight road joins the old roads, passes through P, and meets the second road at A, x km from Q. Find x minimizing the new road length.
Open interactive graph and model
Show complete solution

Collinearity gives the vertical intercept height 1 + 8 / x . Hence L 2 ( x ) = ( x + 8 ) 2 + ( 1 + 8 / x ) 2 , x>0. Differentiating, or differentiating ln L, yields x 3 = 8 ; therefore x=2 km. The minimum road length is 5 5 km.

Model and calculus verification

Road length²

Objective and domainf(x)=(x+8)2(x2+1)x2F{\left(x \right)} = \frac{\left(x + 8\right)^{2} \left(x^{2} + 1\right)}{x^{2}}
0.1≤x≤120.1\le x\le 12
Differentiateⅆⅆxf(x)=2x+16-16x2-128x3\frac{d}{d x} F{\left(x \right)} = 2 x + 16 - \frac{16}{x^{2}} - \frac{128}{x^{3}}
Compare admissible candidates
xF
0.10.16626.616626.61
22125125
1212402.778402.778
Verified min: x=2x = 2. The interactive diagram and graph use this same objective.
Open persistent student workspace
QUESTION 76

Run or Swim?

Question 76Level 2
A dog runs 6.4 m/s and swims 0.9 m/s toward a ball 4 m offshore and 8 m downshore. Find where it should enter the water. Show the same offset rule holds for any downshore distance z>1.
Open interactive graph and model
Show complete solution

Minimize running time plus swimming time. The derivative yields a fixed horizontal swimming component depending on the speed ratio.

Model and calculus verification

Travel time

Objective and domaint(x)=0.15625x+1.11111111111111(x-8)2+16T{\left(x \right)} = 0.15625 x + 1.11111111111111 \sqrt{\left(x - 8\right)^{2} + 16}
0≤x≤80\le x\le 8
Differentiateⅆⅆxt(x)=1.11111111111111x+0.15625x2-16x+80-8.88888888888888x2-16x+80\frac{d}{d x} T{\left(x \right)} = \frac{8.88888888888888 \left(0.125 x + 0.017578125 \sqrt{x^{2} - 16 x + 80} - 1.0\right)}{\sqrt{x^{2} - 16 x + 80}}
Compare admissible candidates
xT
009.938089.93808
7.431857.431855.650285.65028
885.694445.69444
Verified min: x=7.43185x = 7.43185. The interactive diagram and graph use this same objective.

Law of Reflection

Light reflects from a mirror between fixed points A and B. Find the reflection point minimizing distance and prove angle of incidence equals angle of reflection.
Open interactive graph and model
Show complete solution

Differentiate the sum of distances, or reflect one endpoint across the mirror and use a straight line.

QUESTION 78

Ladder Around a Corner

Question 78Level 2
Hallways of widths 5 ft and 4 ft meet at a right angle. Find the longest ladder that can turn the corner, derive the general formula, and solve the inverse-width variants.
Open interactive graph and model
Show complete solution

Minimize L ( θ ) = a csc θ + b sec θ . The minimum is ( a 2 / 3 + b 2 / 3 ) 3 / 2 .

Apple Orchard Density

Question 80Level 2
At 20 trees per feddan, each tree yields 30 boxes. Each extra tree reduces yield per tree by 1 box. Find trees per feddan maximizing total production.
Open interactive graph and model
Show complete solution

Let x be extra trees: T=(20+x)(30−x), maximized at x=5. Plant 25 trees for 625 boxes.

Model and calculus verification

Total production

Worker Efficiency

Question 84Level 2
A worker produces Q(t)=−t3+12t2+60t. Interpret Q′ as efficiency, find when it is maximum, and connect this with diminishing returns.

Efficiency is Q′. Its maximum occurs where Q″=0 and Q″ changes sign, the inflection point of Q.

Profit from Demand and Cost

Question 85Level 2

Open interactive graph and model

Furniture Production Profit

Question 87Level 2

Open interactive graph and model
Show complete solution

Revenue is R(x) = 2800x. Therefore P(x) = R(x) − C(x) = −x3 + 3x2 + 2880x − 5000.

QUESTION 88

Recovering Cost from Profit

Question 88Level 2
A factory sells x units daily at 30 QAR per unit and profit is P(x)=40x−x2−100. Find the production level at which daily cost is smallest.
Open interactive graph and model
Show complete solution

Revenue is R(x) = 30x. Since P = R − C, C(x) = R(x) − P(x) = 30x − (40x − x2 − 100) = x2 − 10x + 100.

Model and calculus verification

Cost

Objective and domainc(x)=x2-10x+100C{\left(x \right)} = x^{2} - 10 x + 100
0≤x≤200\le x\le 20
Differentiateⅆⅆxc(x)=2(x-5)\frac{d}{d x} C{\left(x \right)} = 2 \left(x - 5\right)
Compare admissible candidates
xC
00100100
557575
2020300300
Verified min: x=5x = 5. The interactive diagram and graph use this same objective.

QUESTION 89

Circular-Sector Field

Question 89Level 2
A farmer uses PP metres of fencing to enclose a circular sector of radius rr and central angle θ\theta radians. (a) Prove P=r⁢(θ+2)P=r(\theta+2). (b) Show that A(r)=Pr2−r2A(r)=\frac{Pr}{2}-r^2. (c) Find the value of rr, in terms of PP, that maximizes the area.
Open interactive graph and model
Show complete solution

The perimeter constraint is P=2r+rθ=r(θ+2)P=2r+r\theta=r(\theta+2). Since θ=Pr-2\theta=\frac{P}{r}-2, the sector area becomes A(r)=12r2θ=Pr2−r2A(r)=\frac12r^2\theta=\frac{Pr}{2}-r^2. Thus A′(r)=P2−2rA'(r)=\frac{P}{2}-2r vanishes at r=P4r=\frac{P}{4}, where A″(r)=−2<0A''(r)=-2<0.

Model and calculus verification

Sector area

Objective and domaina(r)=r(10-r)A{\left(r \right)} = r \left(10 - r\right)
0≤r≤100\le r\le 10
Differentiateⅆⅆra(r)=10-2r\frac{d}{d r} A{\left(r \right)} = 10 - 2 r
Compare admissible candidates
rA
0000
552525
101000
Verified max: r=5r = 5. The interactive diagram and graph use this same objective.

Normalized to P=20; in general r=P/4.

Open persistent student workspace

QUESTION 91

Triangle Area Identity Challenge

Question 91Level 2
A triangle has one angle π/4 and opposite side 2 cm. (26) Prove A=sin⁡2θ−cos⁡2θ+1. (27) Prove maximum area is 1+2 cm².
Open interactive graph and model
Show complete solution

Write the area in the equivalent form A(θ)=1+√2 sin(2θ−π/4). Since −1≤sin(2θ−π/4)≤1, the largest possible value is 1+√2. It occurs when 2θ−π/4=π/2, so θ=3π/8 within the geometric interval.

Model and calculus verification

Triangle area

Objective and domaina(θ)=-2cos⁡(2θ+π4)+1A{\left(\theta \right)} = - \sqrt{2} \cos{\left(2 \theta + \frac{\pi}{4} \right)} + 1
0≤θ≤1.57080\le \theta\le 1.5708

Compare admissible candidates
θA
0000

Open interactive graph and model
Show complete solution

QUESTION 97

Windpipe Radius

Question 97Level 2
Air velocity is V(r)=cr²(r₀−r). Find the radius maximizing flow and state whether the windpipe expands or contracts.
Open interactive graph and model
Show complete solution

V ′ = c r ( 2 r 0 − 3 r ) ; the interior maximum is r = 2 r 0 / 3 , a contraction.

QUESTION 101

Minimizing Highway Construction Cost

Question 101Level 3
An interchange is 8 miles east and 8 miles south of a bridge. The first 5 miles south are marsh. Marsh construction costs 10 million QAR/mile and dry land 7 million QAR/mile. Choose the marsh exit point.
Open interactive graph and model
Show complete solution

Numerically, C′(x)=0 at x≈3.560052. C(0)≈109.8, C(8)≈115.3, and C(3.560052)≈98.9 million QAR, so the interior point is the absolute minimum.

Construction cost

Question 102Level 3
A rescuer runs along a beach at speed v₁ and swims at speed v₂. A protected shoreline segment cannot be entered. Formulate the constrained optimization and explain when the optimum occurs at the boundary of the forbidden interval.
Open interactive graph and model
Show complete solution

QUESTION 105

Two-Layer Minimum-Time Path

Question 105Level 3
A signal travels through two horizontal layers. The upper layer has depth 4 units and speed 2 units/s; the lower layer has depth 6 units and speed 1 unit/s. The horizontal separation is 10 units. If x is the horizontal distance travelled in the upper layer, use the Pythagorean theorem and similar right triangles to construct and minimize the total travel time.
Open interactive graph and model
Show complete solution

The two path pieces are hypotenuses of right triangles, so their lengths are d_1=√(x²+4²), d_2=√((10−x)²+6²). Hence T(x)=√(x²+16)/2+√((10−x)²+36), 0≤x≤10. Differentiating gives T′(x)=x/[2√(x²+16)]−(10−x)/√((10−x)²+36). The critical equation is x/√(x²+16)=(10−x)/[2√((10−x)²+36)]. Move the crossing point a small horizontal distance. The small change triangles are similar to the two path triangles, so each path-length change is proportional to horizontal leg divided by hypotenuse. Balancing the two time changes gives the critical equation. Solving it gives x≈7.09666. Comparing with the endpoints confirms the absolute minimum. Minimum-time path: travel horizontally about 7.097 units in the upper layer and 2.903 units in the lower layer.

Model and calculus verification

Total travel time

Objective and domaint(x)=x2+162+(x-10)2+36T{\left(x \right)} = \frac{\sqrt{x^{2} + 16}}{2} + \sqrt{\left(x - 10\right)^{2} + 36}
0≤x≤100\le x\le 10
Differentiateⅆⅆxt(x)=2xx2+16+xx2-20x+136-20x2+162x2+16x2-20x+136\frac{d}{d x} T{\left(x \right)} = \frac{2 x \sqrt{x^{2} + 16} + x \sqrt{x^{2} - 20 x + 136} - 20 \sqrt{x^{2} + 16}}{2 \sqrt{x^{2} + 16} \sqrt{x^{2} - 20 x + 136}}
Compare admissible candidates
xT
0013.661913.6619
7.096667.0966610.738710.7387
101011.385211.3852
Verified min: x=7.09666x = 7.09666. The interactive diagram and graph use this same objective.

Two horizontal layers have depths 4 and 6, horizontal span 10, and speeds 2 and 1. The two right triangles determine the distances.

QUESTION 108

Endpoint Trap with Capacity

Question 108Level 3
A manufacturer has profit P(x)=−x³+12x²−36x on 0≤x≤10, but can only produce whole units. Find the continuous optimizer, then the actual integer optimizer.
Open interactive graph and model
Show complete solution

P′(x)=−3(x−2)(x−6), so the critical numbers are 2 and 6. Compare all candidates: P(0)=0, P(2)=−32, P(6)=0, P(10)=−160. Therefore the continuous absolute maximum is 0 and is attained at both x=0 and x=6. Because both are integers, the integer model has the same two maximizers. If the manufacturer is required to produce a positive number of units, x=6 is the maximizing choice.

Model and calculus verification

Profit

Objective and domainp(x)=x(-x2+12x-36)P{\left(x \right)} = x \left(- x^{2} + 12 x - 36\right)
0≤x≤100\le x\le 10
Differentiateⅆⅆxp(x)=-3x2+24x-36\frac{d}{d x} P{\left(x \right)} = - 3 x^{2} + 24 x - 36
Compare admissible candidates
xP
0000
22-32-32
6600
1010-160-160
Verified max: x=0x = 0; x=6x = 6. The interactive diagram and graph use this same objective.

QUESTION 109

AP FRQ: Table of Marginal Profit

Question 109Level 3

The marginal profit data are modeled by P′(x)=x(10−x), and P(0)=0.

x02468101215
P′(x)0162424160−24−75

Use the table to locate candidates for maximum profit, determine the maximizing production level, and justify the absolute maximum on 0≤x≤15.

Open interactive graph and model
Show complete solution

The table shows P′(x)>0 for 0<x<10 and P′(x)<0 for 10<x≤15. Thus P increases up to x=10 and decreases afterward. P(x)=∫x(10−x)dx=5x²−x³/3+C. Since P(0)=0, C=0. Compare the closed-interval candidates: P(0)=0, P(10)=500/3, P(15)=0. Absolute maximum: P(10)=500/3, attained at production level x=10.

Model and calculus verification

Profit reconstructed from marginal profit

Objective and domainp(x)=x2(15-x)3P{\left(x \right)} = \frac{x^{2} \left(15 - x\right)}{3}
0≤x≤150\le x\le 15
Differentiateⅆⅆxp(x)=-x(x-10)\frac{d}{d x} P{\left(x \right)} = - x \left(x - 10\right)
Compare admissible candidates
xP
0000
1010166.667166.667
151500
Verified max: x=10x = 10. The interactive diagram and graph use this same objective.

Here P′(x)=x(10−x), matching the displayed marginal-profit table, and P(0)=0.

QUESTION 111

Kepler's Cask Problem

Question 111Level 3
For Kepler's straight-sided cask model v(y)=2π-w2+z2(y2+y(w-y)+(w-y)2)3V{\left(y \right)} = \frac{2 \pi \sqrt{- w^{2} + z^{2}} \left(y^{2} + y \left(w - y\right) + \left(w - y\right)^{2}\right)}{3}, treat w and z as constants. Show that the stationary point is y=w/2, classify it, and explain why stationarity reduces first-order volume-estimation error.
Open interactive graph and model
Show complete solution

Let K=(2π/3)√(z²−w²)>0. Expanding the bracket gives V(y)=K(y²−wy+w²). Therefore V′(y)=K(2y−w), V″(y)=2K>0. Thus V′(y)=0 at y=w/2, and the displayed algebraic model forces this point to be a strict minimum. A claim that it is neither a maximum nor a minimum would require an additional geometric dependency or constraint not present in the displayed formula. For a small measurement error Δy near y=w/2, the linear term V′(w/2)Δy vanishes. Consequently the leading error is quadratic: ΔV≈(1/2)V″(w/2)(Δy)². The stationary measurement is first-order insensitive to small errors, and—under the stated formula—it is a minimum.

Model and calculus verification

Normalized straight-sided cask volume

Objective and domainv(y)=25π(y2-2y+4)3V{\left(y \right)} = \frac{2 \sqrt{5} \pi \left(y^{2} - 2 y + 4\right)}{3}
0≤y≤20\le y\le 2
Differentiateⅆⅆyv(y)=45π(y-1)3\frac{d}{d y} V{\left(y \right)} = \frac{4 \sqrt{5} \pi \left(y - 1\right)}{3}
Compare admissible candidates
yV
0018.732818.7328
1114.049614.0496
2218.732818.7328
Verified min: y=1y = 1. The interactive diagram and graph use this same objective.

Normalized with w=2 and z=3. For the displayed formula, y=w/2 is a minimum, not a stationary inflection.

This corrects the classification forced by the displayed algebraic model.

The break-even delay for two 125 ft/s throws is about 0.18770 s. With a 0.1-s delay, the break-even second-throw speed is about 116.05 ft/s.

QUESTION 113

Integer Fence Panels

Question 113Level 3
A river enclosure uses identical 2-m fence panels and must enclose at least 1800 m². Find the least number of panels and dimensions, respecting integer panel counts.
Open interactive graph and model
Show complete solution

Solve the continuous three-sided fence problem first, then test nearby integer panel allocations satisfying the area inequality.

Model and calculus verification

Fence length

Objective and domainc(x)=2x+1800xC{\left(x \right)} = 2 x + \frac{1800}{x}
1≤x≤1001\le x\le 100
Differentiateⅆⅆxc(x)=2-1800x2\frac{d}{d x} C{\left(x \right)} = 2 - \frac{1800}{x^{2}}
Compare admissible candidates
xC
1118021802
3030120120
100100218218
Verified min: x=30x = 30. The interactive diagram and graph use this same objective.

The continuous optimum uses exactly 60 two-metre panels, so it is also integer-feasible.

Open persistent student workspace
QUESTION 114

Asymmetric Rectangle Between Curves

Question 114Level 3
A rectangle has vertical sides at x=−u and x=v. Its upper-left corner lies on y=16−x², its upper-right corner lies on y=16−x²/4, and an internal divider requires v=2u. Find the maximum possible area.
Open interactive graph and model
Show complete solution

At x=−u the height is 16−u². At x=v=2u on the second curve, the height is 16−v²/4=16−u², so the top edge is horizontal. The width is u+v=3u. Hence A(u)=3u(16−u²), 0≤u≤4. Then A′(u)=48−9u², so u=4/√3. The derivative changes from positive to negative, giving the absolute maximum. The width is 4√3 and the height is 32/3.

Model and calculus verification

Area

Objective and domaina(u)=3u(16-u2)A{\left(u \right)} = 3 u \left(16 - u^{2}\right)
0≤u≤40\le u\le 4
Differentiateⅆⅆua(u)=48-9u2\frac{d}{d u} A{\left(u \right)} = 48 - 9 u^{2}
Compare admissible candidates
uA
0000
433\frac{4 \sqrt{3}}{3}73.900873.9008
4400
Verified max: u=433u = \frac{4 \sqrt{3}}{3}. The interactive diagram and graph use this same objective.
Open persistent student workspace
QUESTION 115

Weighted Open Box

Question 115Level 3
A square-based open box must hold volume V. The base material costs three times as much per unit area as the side material. Find the optimal ratio h:x and prove the minimizer is absolute.
Open interactive graph and model
Show complete solution

Cost is 3x²+4xh with h=V/x², so C=3x²+4V/x. C′=6x−4V/x²=0 gives x³=2V/3 and h/x=3/2.

Model and calculus verification

Weighted cost

Objective and domainc(x)=3x3+4xC{\left(x \right)} = \frac{3 x^{3} + 4}{x}
0.15≤x≤30.15\le x\le 3
Differentiateⅆⅆxc(x)=6x-4x2\frac{d}{d x} C{\left(x \right)} = 6 x - \frac{4}{x^{2}}
Compare admissible candidates
xC
320\frac{3}{20}26.734226.7342
0.873580.873586.868296.86829
3328.333328.3333
Verified min: x=0.87358x = 0.87358. The interactive diagram and graph use this same objective.

Normalized to volume V=1.

QUESTION 117

Minimum Surface Area with a Lid Ratio

Question 117Level 3
A closed box has square base, volume 500 cm³, and a lid whose material is twice as expensive as the base and sides. Find the dimensions minimizing cost.
Open interactive graph and model
Show complete solution

Cost-weighted area is 3x²+4xh, since base weight1 and lid weight2. Substitute h=500/x² and optimize.

QUESTION 118

Closest Point to an Exponential

Question 118Level 3
Find the point on y=ex closest to (0,2). Prove uniqueness of the minimizer.
Open interactive graph and model
Show complete solution

Minimize D²=x²+(e^x−2)². The equation x+e^x(e^x−2)=0 has a unique relevant root because the derivative of the left side is positive near and beyond the crossing; solve numerically.

Model and calculus verification

Distance²

Objective and domaind(x)=x2+(ⅇx-2)2D{\left(x \right)} = x^{2} + \left(e^{x} - 2\right)^{2}
-2≤x≤2-2\le x\le 2
Differentiateⅆⅆxd(x)=2(x+ⅇ2x-2ⅇx)\frac{d}{d x} D{\left(x \right)} = 2 \left(x + e^{2 x} - 2 e^{x}\right)
Compare admissible candidates
xD

QUESTION 119

Piecewise Ticket Demand

Question 119Level 3
Let dd be the discount, measured in QAR. The demand is N(d)={1000+50d0≤d≤81400d≥8N(d)=\begin{cases}1000+50d,&0\le d\le 8,\\1400,&d\ge 8.\end{cases}Each customer generates 30-d30-d QAR in total revenue. Find the global maximum revenue and the value of dd at which it occurs.
Open interactive graph and model
Show complete solution

On d≥0∧d≤80\le d\le 8, R(d)=(30−d)(1000+50d)R(d)=(30-d)(1000+50d). Its vertex occurs at d=5d=5. For d≥8d\ge 8, R(d)=1400(30−d)R(d)=1400(30-d) is decreasing. Comparing the branch maximum and the junction gives the global maximum 3125031\,250 QAR at d=5d=5.

Model and calculus verification

Piecewise revenue

Objective and domainr(d)=piecewise((-50(d-30)(d+20),d≤8),(42000-1400d,True))R{\left(d \right)} = \begin{cases} - 50 \left(d - 30\right) \left(d + 20\right) & \text{for}\: d \leq 8 \\42000 - 1400 d & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
0≤d≤300\le d\le 30
Differentiateⅆⅆdr(d)=piecewise((500-100d,d≤8),(-1400,True))\frac{d}{d d} R{\left(d \right)} = \begin{cases} 500 - 100 d & \text{for}\: d \leq 8 \\-1400 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
Compare admissible candidates
dR
003000030000
553125031250
883080030800
303000
Verified max: d=5d = 5. The interactive diagram and graph use this same objective.
Open persistent student workspace
QUESTION 120

Profit with a Fixed Setup Cost

Question 120Level 3
Explain why adding a fixed setup cost K to a cost function does not change the profit-maximizing production level, but can change whether production should occur at all.
Open interactive graph and model
Show complete solution

K shifts profit downward without changing P′. The interior optimizer is unchanged, but if its maximum profit becomes negative, the firm should choose zero production when shutdown is allowed.

Model and calculus verification

Profit without setup cost

Objective and domainp(x)=20-(x-5)2P{\left(x \right)} = 20 - \left(x - 5\right)^{2}
0≤x≤100\le x\le 10
Differentiateⅆⅆxp(x)=10-2x\frac{d}{d x} P{\left(x \right)} = 10 - 2 x
Compare admissible candidates
xP
00-5-5
552020
1010-5-5
Verified max: x=5x = 5. The interactive diagram and graph use this same objective.

Profit with K=25

0≤x≤100\le x\le 10

Compare admissible candidates

00-30-30

24 questions
QUESTION 121

Square Maximizes Area

Question 121Practice & Homework
Prove that among rectangles of perimeter P, the square has maximum area.
Open interactive graph and model
Show complete solution

Let x+y=P/2. Then A = x ( P / 2 − x ) , a concave quadratic maximized at x=y=P/4.

Model and calculus verification

Area

Objective and domaina(x)=x(20-x)A{\left(x \right)} = x \left(20 - x\right)
0≤x≤200\le x\le 20
Differentiateⅆⅆxa(x)=20-2x\frac{d}{d x} A{\left(x \right)} = 20 - 2 x
Compare admissible candidates
xA
0000
1010100100
202000
Verified max: x=10x = 10. The interactive diagram and graph use this same objective.

Normalized to perimeter P=40.

Open persistent student workspace
QUESTION 122

Square Minimizes Perimeter

Question 122Practice & Homework
Prove that among rectangles of area A, the square has minimum perimeter.
Open interactive graph and model
Show complete solution

Let the rectangle sides be x and A/x , where x>0 . Then P(x)=2x+2A/x. Differentiating gives P′(x)=2−2A/x². Thus P′(x)=0 exactly when x²=A, so x=√A. Since P′′(x)=4A/x³>0, this point gives the absolute minimum. Therefore both sides are √A and the minimizing rectangle is a square.

Model and calculus verification

Perimeter

Objective and domainc(x)=2x+200xC{\left(x \right)} = 2 x + \frac{200}{x}
1≤x≤501\le x\le 50
Differentiateⅆⅆxc(x)=2-200x2\frac{d}{d x} C{\left(x \right)} = 2 - \frac{200}{x^{2}}
Compare admissible candidates
xC
11202202
10104040
5050104104
Verified min: x=10x = 10. The interactive diagram and graph use this same objective.

Normalized to area A=100.

Open persistent student workspace

QUESTION 126

Rectangle in a Right Isosceles Triangle

Question 126Practice & Homework
A rectangle is inscribed in the coordinate right isosceles triangle shown, whose base has length 2. (5) Find y-coordinate of P in terms of x. (6) Write area in terms of x. (7) Find maximum area. (8) Find dimensions.
Open interactive graph and model
Show complete solution

With A=(-1,0), B=(0,1), C=(1,0), the side is y=1−x. Thus A=2x(1−x), maximized at x=1/2; dimensions 1 by 1/2.

Open Box from an 8 dm Square

Question 127Practice & Homework

Open interactive graph and model
Show complete solution

V ′ = 12 x 2 − 64 x + 64 = 4 ( 3 x − 4 ) ( x − 4 ) The admissible interior point is x=4/3 dm. The box dimensions are 16/3 dm × 16/3 dm × 4/3 dm and Vmax=1024/27 dm³.

QUESTION 128

Open Box: 6×10 Sheet

Question 128Practice & Homework
Cut x-inch squares from a 6 in × 10 in sheet and fold an open box. Find x maximizing volume.
Open interactive graph and model
Show complete solution

The physical domain is 0<x<3. The volume is V(x)=x(6−2x)(10−2x). Then V′(x)=12x²−64x+60=4(3x²−16x+15). The roots are x=(8±√19)/3, but only x=(8−√19)/3≈1.2137 lies in (0,3). The derivative changes from positive to negative there, while V tends to 0 at both endpoints. Hence this value gives the absolute maximum.

Model and calculus verification

Volume

Objective and domainv(x)=4x(x-5)(x-3)V{\left(x \right)} = 4 x \left(x - 5\right) \left(x - 3\right)
0≤x≤30\le x\le 3
Differentiateⅆⅆxv(x)=12x2-64x+60\frac{d}{d x} V{\left(x \right)} = 12 x^{2} - 64 x + 60
Compare admissible candidates
xV
0000
1.21371.213732.835332.8353
3300
Verified max: x=1.2137x = 1.2137. The interactive diagram and graph use this same objective.
Open persistent student workspace

Open interactive graph and model
Show complete solution

The box has base dimensions (12 − 2x) × (12 − 2x) and height x: V (x) = x(12 − 2x)2 , 0 < x < 6. The interior critical point is x = 2, which gives the maximum.

SECTION 05
02

Reasoning and Reflection

Explain why optimization procedures work before beginning longer computations.

7 questions
QUESTION 133

Endpoint or Critical Point?

Question 133Reasoning
A continuous objective is defined on a closed physical interval. Explain why the absolute optimum must be chosen from the endpoints and the interior critical numbers. Use the displayed area model to illustrate the argument.
Open interactive graph and model
Show complete solution

The closed-interval method requires every endpoint and every interior point where the derivative is zero or undefined. A local derivative test alone cannot rule out a larger endpoint value.

Model and calculus verification

Area

Objective and domaina(x)=x(20-x)A{\left(x \right)} = x \left(20 - x\right)
0≤x≤200\le x\le 20
Differentiateⅆⅆxa(x)=20-2x\frac{d}{d x} A{\left(x \right)} = 20 - 2 x
Compare admissible candidates
xA
0000
1010100100
202000
Verified max: x=10x = 10. The interactive diagram and graph use this same objective.

Endpoints and interior critical points are all candidates on a closed interval.

Open persistent student workspace
QUESTION 134

Choose the Better Variable

Question 134Reasoning
For a cylinder inscribed in a cone of radius 5 and height 9, use similar triangles to write the volume first as a function of cylinder height h and then as a function of cylinder radius r. Explain why both models must give the same physical optimum.
Open interactive graph and model
Show complete solution

Similar triangles give r/5=(9-h)/9 and h/9=(5-r)/5. Substitution produces equivalent one-variable objectives, so their optimizing dimensions describe the same cylinder.

Model and calculus verification

Height model

Objective and domainf(h)=25πh(h-9)281F{\left(h \right)} = \frac{25 \pi h \left(h - 9\right)^{2}}{81}
0≤h≤90\le h\le 9
Differentiateⅆⅆhf(h)=25π(h-9)(h-3)27\frac{d}{d h} F{\left(h \right)} = \frac{25 \pi \left(h - 9\right) \left(h - 3\right)}{27}
Compare admissible candidates
hF
0000
33104.72104.72
9900
Verified max: h=3h = 3. The interactive diagram and graph use this same objective.

Cylinder in a cone, using height as the variable.

Radius model

Objective and domainf(r)=9πr2(5-r)5F{\left(r \right)} = \frac{9 \pi r^{2} \left(5 - r\right)}{5}
0≤r≤50\le r\le 5
Differentiateⅆⅆrf(r)=-9πr(3r-10)5\frac{d}{d r} F{\left(r \right)} = - \frac{9 \pi r \left(3 r - 10\right)}{5}
Compare admissible candidates
rF
0000
3.333333.33333104.72104.72
5500
Verified max: r=3.33333r = 3.33333. The interactive diagram and graph use this same objective.

The same geometry, using radius as the variable.

Similar triangles make the two one-variable models equivalent.

QUESTION 135

Physical Domain Before Differentiation

Question 135Reasoning
Squares of side x are removed from a 12 by 16 sheet to form an open box. Determine the physical domain before differentiating, and explain why an algebraic critical point outside that interval must be rejected.
Open interactive graph and model
Show complete solution

The base dimensions are 12-2x and 16-2x, so 0≤x≤6. Calculus candidates are admissible only when every physical dimension is nonnegative.

Model and calculus verification

Box volume

Objective and domainv(x)=4x(x-8)(x-6)V{\left(x \right)} = 4 x \left(x - 8\right) \left(x - 6\right)
0≤x≤60\le x\le 6
Differentiateⅆⅆxv(x)=4(3x2-28x+48)\frac{d}{d x} V{\left(x \right)} = 4 \left(3 x^{2} - 28 x + 48\right)
Compare admissible candidates
xV
0000
2.262972.26297194.067194.067

QUESTION 136

Continuous Model, Discrete Decision

Question 136Reasoning
An optimization model has a continuous maximizer x=17.4, but x must represent a whole number of panels. Describe the correct procedure for choosing the actual design.
Open interactive graph and model
Show complete solution

The derivative locates the continuous optimum. The discrete optimum must then be found by comparing the feasible integers nearest 17.4, together with any relevant boundary values.

Model and calculus verification

Continuous objective

Objective and domainf(x)=400-(x-17.4)2F{\left(x \right)} = 400 - \left(x - 17.4\right)^{2}
10≤x≤2510\le x\le 25
Differentiateⅆⅆxf(x)=34.8-2x\frac{d}{d x} F{\left(x \right)} = 34.8 - 2 x

17.417.4400400
2525342.24342.24
Verified max: x=17.4x = 17.4. The interactive diagram and graph use this same objective.

QUESTION 137

Why Squared Distance Works

Question 137Reasoning
Explain why a closest-point problem may minimize D² instead of D. Then show how the derivative condition leads to a shortest segment perpendicular to the tangent when the minimizer is an interior point.
Open interactive graph and model
Show complete solution

Because the square-root function is strictly increasing on nonnegative inputs, D and D² have identical minimizers. Differentiating D² makes the tangent vector orthogonal to the joining vector at an interior optimum.

Model and calculus verification

Squared distance

Objective and domaind(x)=(x-3)2+(x2-4)2D{\left(x \right)} = \left(x - 3\right)^{2} + \left(x^{2} - 4\right)^{2}
-2≤x≤3-2\le x\le 3
Differentiateⅆⅆxd(x)=4x3-14x-6\frac{d}{d x} D{\left(x \right)} = 4 x^{3} - 14 x - 6
Compare admissible candidates
xD
-2-22525
-1.60096-1.6009623.233623.2336
-0.455589-0.45558926.323726.3237
2.056552.056550.9427210.942721
332525
Verified min: x=2.05655x = 2.05655. The interactive diagram and graph use this same objective.

Minimizing D² avoids a square root.

Distance

Objective and domaind(x)=(x-3)2+(x2-4)2D{\left(x \right)} = \sqrt{\left(x - 3\right)^{2} + \left(x^{2} - 4\right)^{2}}
-2≤x≤3-2\le x\le 3

-2-255
-1.60096-1.600964.820124.82012

Verified min: x=2.05655x = 2.05655. The interactive diagram and graph use this same objective.

D and D² have the same minimizers because √u is increasing for u≥0.

Open persistent student workspace
QUESTION 138

Sensitivity and Rounding

Question 138Reasoning
Suppose F′(x₀)=0. Use a second-order approximation to explain why a small rounding error near x₀ often changes the objective only slightly. State when rounding up or down may still violate the physical constraint.
Open interactive graph and model
Show complete solution

Near a stationary optimum, F(x₀+Δ)≈F(x₀)+(1/2)F″(x₀)Δ². The error is second order, but a one-sided material, capacity, or safety constraint can still determine the permitted direction of rounding.

Model and calculus verification

Robust cost

Objective and domainc(x)=(x-4)450+3(x-4)210+5C{\left(x \right)} = \frac{\left(x - 4\right)^{4}}{50} + \frac{3 \left(x - 4\right)^{2}}{10} + 5
0≤x≤80\le x\le 8
Differentiateⅆⅆxc(x)=(x-4)(2x2-16x+47)25\frac{d}{d x} C{\left(x \right)} = \frac{\left(x - 4\right) \left(2 x^{2} - 16 x + 47\right)}{25}
Compare admissible candidates
xC
0014.9214.92
4455
8814.9214.92
Verified min: x=4x = 4. The interactive diagram and graph use this same objective.

Near a stationary optimum the leading penalty is usually quadratic in the implementation error.

Open persistent student workspace
QUESTION 139

Audit the Model Before Calculus

Question 139Reasoning
A rectangle is divided into three sections by two internal fences parallel to its width. With 1000 m of fencing, decide whether the correct constraint is 2L+2W=1000 or 2L+4W=1000. Explain how the wrong count changes the optimizer.
Open interactive graph and model
Show complete solution

There are two outside lengths and four width segments: two outer sides plus two internal dividers. Therefore 2L+4W=1000. Omitting the internal fences creates a different and incorrect objective.

Model and calculus verification

Correct three-section area

Objective and domaina(W)=2W(250-W)A{\left(W \right)} = 2 W \left(250 - W\right)
0≤W≤2500\le W\le 250
DifferentiateⅆⅆWa(W)=500-4W\frac{d}{d W} A{\left(W \right)} = 500 - 4 W
Compare admissible candidates
WA
0000
1251253125031250
25025000
Verified max: W=125W = 125. The interactive diagram and graph use this same objective.

Correct constraint: 2L+4W=1000.

Incorrect model for comparison

Objective and domainf(W)=W(500-W)F{\left(W \right)} = W \left(500 - W\right)
0≤W≤5000\le W\le 500
DifferentiateⅆⅆWf(W)=500-2W\frac{d}{d W} F{\left(W \right)} = 500 - 2 W
Compare admissible candidates
WF
0000
2502506250062500
50050000
Verified max: W=250W = 250. The interactive diagram and graph use this same objective.

This flawed model forgets the two internal fences.

Use the diagram to audit the fence count before differentiating.

QUESTION 142

Perpendicularity in Examples 7.3–7.4

Question 142Practice & Homework
Repeat the perpendicularity proof for the two parabola-distance examples.
Open interactive graph and model
Show complete solution

A point on y=9−x² is Q=(x,9−x²), and let the fixed point be A=(a,b). Then D²(x)=(x−a)²+(9−x²−b)². At a minimizing point, 0=(D²)′=2(x−a)−4x(9−x²−b), hence x−a=2x(9−x²−b). The joining-line slope is m AQ =(9−x²−b)/(x−a)=1/(2x), and the tangent slope to the parabola is −2x. Their product is −1. The two models correspond to A=(3,9) and A=(5,11). In both examples, the minimum-distance segment is normal to the parabola.

Model and calculus verification

Distance² from (3,9) to y=9−x²

Objective and domaind(x)=x4+(x-3)2D{\left(x \right)} = x^{4} + \left(x - 3\right)^{2}
-2.5≤x≤2.5-2.5\le x\le 2.5
Differentiateⅆⅆxd(x)=4x3+2x-6\frac{d}{d x} D{\left(x \right)} = 4 x^{3} + 2 x - 6
Compare admissible candidates
xD
-52- \frac{5}{2}69.312569.3125
1155
52\frac{5}{2}39.312539.3125
Verified min: x=1x = 1. The interactive diagram and graph use this same objective.

Distance² from (5,11) to y=9−x²

Objective and domaind(x)=(x-5)2+(x2+2)2D{\left(x \right)} = \left(x - 5\right)^{2} + \left(x^{2} + 2\right)^{2}
-2≤x≤2-2\le x\le 2
Differentiateⅆⅆxd(x)=4x3+10x-10\frac{d}{d x} D{\left(x \right)} = 4 x^{3} + 10 x - 10
Compare admissible candidates
xD
-2-28585
0.7972810.79728124.609524.6095
224545
Verified min: x=0.797281x = 0.797281. The interactive diagram and graph use this same objective.

QUESTION 143

Water-Line Junction

Question 143Practice & Homework
Two developments lie 4 miles and 5 miles south of an east-west line, with 5 miles horizontal separation. Find the single connection point minimizing total new line.
Open interactive graph and model
Show complete solution

Let x be the horizontal position of the junction and minimize the sum of two square-root distances.

Total line

QUESTION 144

Highway Across Marsh

Question 144Practice & Homework
A bridge and interchange are 8 miles east and 10 miles south apart; the first 4 miles are marsh. Marsh road costs 5 million/mile and dry road 2 million/mile. Find the optimal exit point and savings.
Open interactive graph and model
Show complete solution

Use a two-segment weighted-distance model; compare with the direct straight-line route.

Reestimated Highway Costs

Open interactive graph and model
Show complete solution

Reoptimize the same cost function with changed coefficients, then evaluate both old and new routes.

QUESTION 146

General Run-Swim Ratio

Question 146Practice & Homework
Show that for any offshore distance x, the optimal entry offset is approximately 0.144x.
Open interactive graph and model
Show complete solution

The derivative gives a similarity relation; isolate the horizontal swimming component as a constant multiple of x.

Model and calculus verification

Travel time

Objective and domaint(x)=0.15625x+1.11111111111111(x-8)2+16T{\left(x \right)} = 0.15625 x + 1.11111111111111 \sqrt{\left(x - 8\right)^{2} + 16}
0≤x≤80\le x\le 8
Differentiateⅆⅆxt(x)=1.11111111111111x+0.15625x2-16x+80-8.88888888888888x2-16x+80\frac{d}{d x} T{\left(x \right)} = \frac{8.88888888888888 \left(0.125 x + 0.017578125 \sqrt{x^{2} - 16 x + 80} - 1.0\right)}{\sqrt{x^{2} - 16 x + 80}}
Compare admissible candidates
xT
009.938089.93808
7.431857.431855.650285.65028
885.694445.69444
Verified min: x=7.43185x = 7.43185. The interactive diagram and graph use this same objective.

For offshore distance 4, the optimal horizontal swimming offset is about 0.576=0.144(4).

QUESTION 151

Sector with Fixed Perimeter

Question 151Practice & Homework
A circular sector has perimeter 20 m. Find its radius when its area is as large as possible.
Open interactive graph and model
Show complete solution

Let the radius be r and the angle be θ radians. The perimeter condition is 2r + rθ = 20 =⇒ θ = 20 r − 2.

Model and calculus verification

Sector area
